items.notes: The general word for food or a cooked meal. Тамак жеңиз means 'please eat' and is a standard host invitation. Тамак ичүү literally means eating-drinking and can refer to a meal event.
The round flatbread central to Kyrgyz meals. Нан is never placed on the floor or turned upside down — it carries cultural significance as a staple and sacred food.

Tea is central to Kyrgyz hospitality. Guests are offered чай almost immediately upon arriving. Accepting tea is polite; refusing can seem cold. Кара чай is plain black tea; ак чай (white tea) has milk.
The word for meat generally. Mutton and beef are most common. Эт a common part of many Kyrgyz dish names such as бешбармак (big five-finger dish with meat and noodles).
The general word for fruit. Apricots, apples, and melons are common in Kyrgyz markets. Мөмө жечи — would you like some fruit — is a warm host offer.
First-person singular present tense of каалоо (to want, to wish). In polite speech you can soften it by adding өтүнөм before it or by using the -чы suffix: Чай берчи (please give tea).
Literally 'I see/find it good'. The standard way to say you like something. Мен нан жакшы көрөм — I like bread. More positive than just каалайм; it expresses genuine liking.
The negated form of жактыруу. More direct than жакшы көрбөйм. Useful for politely declining food you genuinely dislike, but soften it with a smile and рахмат.
Used as acceptance to an offer. When someone offers you tea, болот is a friendly way to say yes please. Болбойт means no / it won't work, used for soft refusals.
Literally 'earth apple'. Potatoes are a staple in Kyrgyz cooking. The compound word mirrors French pomme de terre and German Erdapfel — a useful memory hook.
Dairy features strongly in Kyrgyz food culture. Сүт is used in tea, as a drink, and in many fermented products. The ü vowel is front rounded — round your lips.
